hero_sub: Habits are the compound interest of self-improvement. Understand the neurobiology of behavior to design a life of effortless excellence.
definition: A habit is a routine or behavior that is performed regularly and, in many cases, automatically. It is the brain's way of conserving energy by turning repetitive tasks into neural loops consisting of a cue, a craving, a response, and a reward.

why_cards.title: Compound Growth
why_cards.desc: Small 1% improvements every day result in being 37 times better by the end of the year through the power of compounding.

why_cards.title: Cognitive Ease
why_cards.desc: By automating basic behaviors, you free up mental bandwidth for complex problem-solving and creative thinking.

why_cards.title: Identity Shift
why_cards.desc: True behavior change is identity change. Every action you take is a vote for the type of person you wish to become.

why_cards.title: Predictable Success
why_cards.desc: You do not rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems. Habits are the building blocks of systems.

pillars.title: The Habit Loop
pillars.desc: Understanding the Cue, Craving, Response, and Reward cycle is essential for manipulating and mastering any behavior.

pillars.title: Environment Design
pillars.desc: Make good habits obvious and bad habits invisible. Your environment is a stronger driver of behavior than willpower.

pillars.title: The 2-Minute Rule
pillars.desc: When you start a new habit, it should take less than two minutes to do. Optimize for the starting line, not the finish line.

case_studies.name: British Cycling — Marginal Gains
case_studies.desc: Sir Dave Brailsford transformed British cycling by looking for 1% improvements in everything from tire grip to the pillows athletes slept on.
case_studies.lesson: Aggregating small, almost imperceptible improvements across many areas leads to radical transformation over time.

case_studies.name: Jerry Seinfeld — Don't Break the Chain
case_studies.desc: Seinfeld used a wall calendar and a red marker to mark every day he wrote jokes. His only goal was to not break the chain of red X's.
case_studies.lesson: Visual progress tracking provides immediate rewards and reinforces the identity of someone who shows up every day.

lessons.title: Make it Obvious.
lessons.desc: Use implementation intentions: 'I will [BEHAVIOR] at [TIME] in [LOCATION].' Explicit plans increase follow-through.

lessons.title: Use Habit Stacking.
lessons.desc: Pair a new habit with a current one: 'After I [CURRENT HABIT], I will [NEW HABIT].' Leverage existing neural pathways.

lessons.title: Optimize your Environment.
lessons.desc: Place the cues for good habits in front of you and hide the triggers for bad habits. Design for the path of least resistance.

lessons.title: Make it Attractive.
lessons.desc: Use temptation bundling. Pair an action you need to do with an action you want to do to create a dopamine spike.

lessons.title: Make it Easy.
lessons.desc: Reduce friction for good habits and increase friction for bad ones. Make it so easy you can't say no.

lessons.title: Master the Decisive Moment.
lessons.desc: Identify the small choices that deliver outsized impact, like putting on your running shoes or opening your book.

lessons.title: Make it Satisfying.
lessons.desc: The brain prioritizes immediate rewards over delayed ones. Use a habit tracker to make the progress itself feel rewarding.

lessons.title: Never Miss Twice.
lessons.desc: Missing once is an accident. Missing twice is the start of a new habit. Get back on track immediately.

lessons.title: Focus on Systems, Not Goals.
lessons.desc: Goals are about the results you want to achieve. Systems are about the processes that lead to those results.

lessons.title: Embrace Identity-Based Habits.
lessons.desc: Instead of saying 'I want to lose weight,' say 'I am the type of person who never misses a workout.'

power_line: The quality of our lives often depends on the quality of our habits. We are what we repeatedly do.
sources: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones.
Duhigg, C. (2012). The Power of Habit: Why We Do What We Do in Life and Business.
Fogg, B.J. (2019). Tiny Habits: The Small Changes That Change Everything.
Eyal, N. (2014). Hooked: How to Build Habit-Forming Products.
